coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient fluorescence switching of carbon nanodots by CO2
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Xiaoyan
Xiong, Dazhen
Fan, Jing
Li, Zhiyong
Wang, Huiyong
Wang, Jianji
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention in many applications because of their superior optical and chemical properties. To manipulate CDs properties for their smart applications, CDs with CO2 switchable luminescence property have been designed and prepared in the present work. It is found that fluorescence of the polyamine-functionalized CDs can be switched “off” and “on” in DMSO-water mixture by alternatively bubbling and removal of CO2. 13C NMR spectra suggest that the mechanism behind the fluorescence “on-off” results involves the reversible acid-base reaction of the functionalized CDs with CO2 and the formation of hydrophilic polyammonium bicarbonates. Due to the poor solubility of polyammonium bicarbonates in DMSO-water mixture, the formation of the polyammonium bicarbonates precipitation, by CO2 bubbling, decreases the concentration of the functionalized CDs in aqueous DMSO, leading to the switch off of the fluorescence. When CO2 is removed by N2 bubbling, the precipitation is returned to the DMSO-water mixture, and thereby the fluorescence is switched on again. Based on this novel CO2-switchable photoluminescence property, the functionalized CDs have been used for the quantitative detection of CO2 and SO2 in aqueous solution and in the air.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reversible fluorescence “on-off” process of the PEI-CDs
Characterization of structure and morphology of the PEI-CDs
CO2 switchable fluorescence of the PEI-CDs
Possible mechanism for CO2 switchable fluorescence of the PEI-CDs
Reversibility of the CO2 switchable fluorescence of the PEI-CDs
Application of the PEI-CDs for detection of CO2 and SO2

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: key Scientific Research Project Fund of Education Department of Henan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21673068 and 21603063) and the key Scientific Research Project Fund of Education Department of Henan Province (No. 16A150014).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of PEI-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, PEI1.8K and PEI1.8K-CD (a), and FTIR spectra of CDs, PEI1.8K and PEI1.8K-CD at their pure state (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of CDs and PEI1.8K-CD (a), and high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s of PEI1.8K-CD (b–d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
HRTEM images of the CDs (a) and PEI1.8K-CD (b).

The effect of CO2 bubbling on the excitation and emission spectra of PEI1.8K-CD (4 mg/ml) in DMSO-water mixture (wt/wt, 1.5/1.0). Inset: photographs of PL intensity of PEI1.8K-CD before and after CO2 bubbling. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

13C NMR spectra of PEI1.8K-CD before bubbling CO2 (black), after bubbling CO2 (blue), and after the removal of CO2 by bubbling N2 for 30 min at 65 °C (red) (a); Possible mechanism accounting for the switch off and switch on of the fluorescence of PEI-CDs in DMSO-water mixture (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Switch on (black) and switch off (red) of the PL intensity of PEI-CDs at 25 °C by alternatively bubbling and removal of CO2 in 20 cycles with the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence intensity changes of PEI1.8K-CD in DMSO-water mixture upon bubbling different volumes of CO2 at 25 °C. Inset shows the linear plot of PL intensity of PEI1.8K-CD solution as a function of the CO2 concentration. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Highly efficient fluorescence switching of carbon nanodots by CO2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals
Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention in many applications because of their superior optical and chemical properties. To manipulate CDs properties for their smart applications, CDs with CO2 switchable luminescence property have been designed and prepared in the present work. It is found that fluorescence of the polyamine-functionalized CDs can be switched “off” and “on” in DMSO-water mixture by alternatively bubbling and removal of CO2. 13C NMR spectra suggest that the mechanism behind the fluorescence “on-off” results involves the reversible acid-base reaction of the functionalized CDs with CO2 and the formation of hydrophilic polyammonium bicarbonates. Due to the poor solubility of polyammonium bicarbonates in DMSO-water mixture, the formation of the polyammonium bicarbonates precipitation, by CO2 bubbling, decreases the concentration of the functionalized CDs in aqueous DMSO, leading to the switch off of the fluorescence. When CO2 is removed by N2 bubbling, the precipitation is returned to the DMSO-water mixture, and thereby the fluorescence is switched on again. Based on this novel CO2-switchable photoluminescence property, the functionalized CDs have been used for the quantitative detection of CO2 and SO2 in aqueous solution and in the air.

Fluorescence of the PEI functionalized carbon nanodots can be switched “off” and “on” in DMSO-water mixture by alternatively bubbling and removal of CO2, and this novel property has been used for the quantitative detection of CO2 and SO2 in aqueous solution and in air.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) have drawn increasing attention owing to their superior properties such as easy preparation, chemical inertness, resistant to photobleaching, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [1]. These unique properties render CDs numerous potential applications in sensors [2,3], catalysis [4,5], bioimaging [6,7], and drug delivery [8,9]. Typically, CDs contain carboxylic acid or amine groups at the surface, which makes them facile functionalization. Through grafting different stimuli-responsive compounds on their surface, CDs can be designed into functionalized carbon nanomaterials [10,11].

In this context, some excellent works have been reported in recent years. For example, Mao et al. [12] prepared polyaniline functionalized CD composite and investigated its pH-switched photoluminescence and pH sensing properties. Chen et al. [13] reported that organosilane functionalized CDs showed a reversible photoluminescent response toward temperature. Yin et al. [14] found that polyethylenimine-isobutyric amide functionalized CDs were responsive to temperature, pH, salt and organic guests. Sun and his co-workers [10] developed cyanine dye functionalized CDs responsive to SO2 and then used them for selective and quantitative determination of SO2 and its derivatives. More recently, Wang et al. [15] synthesized ionic liquid functionalized CDs with tunable amphiphilicity, and promising applications were demonstrated as ion sensors and fluorescent inks.

CO2 is inexpensive, non-toxic, and easily removable, thus it can be used as a trigger to switch many processes [16,17]. Amine-containing polymers such as polyethyleneimine (PEI) are commercially available and strongly responsive to CO2 [18]. However, it is reported that although hyperbranched PEI has been utilized as surface passivation agent for the luminescent CDs [14,19], the synthesized amine-coated CDs by the pyrolysis of the mixture of carbon source and modifier PEI are almost not responsive to CO2, mainly due to the carbonization of polymer-like CDs at high temperature [20]. To the best of our knowledge, CO2-responsive CDs, which display CO2/N2 switchable fluorescence, have not been reported.

Herein, three commercially available PEI polymers with different molecular weight (Mn = 600, 1800, 10000 g/mol) were employed to prepare PEI functionalized CDs (PEI-CDs) by integrating with PEI polymers on the surface of CDs. CO2-switchable fluorescence of the PEI-CDs in aqueous DMSO solutions was studied by means of fluorescence spectrum, and the mechanism for the fluorescence “on-off” of the systems was investigated by carbon-13 nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR) spectroscopy. Based on this novel CO2-switchable photoluminescence property, the functionalized CDs have been used to quantitatively detect acid gas such as CO2 and SO2 in aqueous solution and in the air.

Citric acid (99%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), and hyperbranched polyethylenimines PEI0.6K (Mn = 600 g/mol), PEI1.8K (Mn = 1800 g/mol) and PEI10K (Mn = 10000 g/mol) were purchased from Aladdin. 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99%) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were acquired from J&K Scientific Ltd. and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., respectively. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiment. CO2 (99.998%), SO2 (99.9%), and N2 (99.9993%) were purchased from Beijing Praxair Application Gas Corp. In the detection experiments of trace amounts of SO2, we diluted pure SO2 by N2 to obtain different volume fractions of SO2. All glassware was cleaned with doubly distilled water and then dried before use.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were determined by using a TU-1900 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 4700 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron energy spectrometer using Al Kα (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray excitation source. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken using a JEOL 2010 electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) measurements were performed with a Netzsch STA 449C thermal analyzer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by using a FLS 980 fluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd). Fluorescent changes were observed under irradiation of a UV lamp (λex = 365 nm). 13C NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker spectrometer (400 MHz) in D2O with tetramethylsilane (TMS) as the standard.

Citric acid coated CDs were firstly prepared by following the hydrothermal procedure reported by Wang et al. [15]. Briefly, citric acid (3.0 g) was added to 3 ml of water in a small round-bottomed flask fitted with a reflux condenser, and the mixture was refluxed for 3 h at 180 °C under argon and magnetic stirring. Then, a brown gel was obtained to show the formation of CDs, and the pristine CDs were dialyzed by a dialysis bag (200 Da) against water for 3 days to remove any precusors.

For the preparation of PEI functionalized CDs, three kinds of commercially available PEIs, PEI0.6K, PEI1.8K, and PEI10K were chemically bonded on the surface of the citric acid coated CDs by a condensation reaction (see Fig. 1 ) to obtain PEI0.6K-CD, PEI1.8K-CD and PEI10K-CD, respectively. In a typical procedure for the preparation of PEI1.8K-CD, 5 mL of the as-prepared citric acid coated CD in DMF (0.4 g/ml) was mixed with EDC/NHS (0.99g/0.60 g) in a 25 mL flask. After stirring for 15 min, another 5 ml of DMF solution of PEI1.8K was injected into the flask. The resulting mixture was kept stirring for 12 h in the dark. Then, it was dialyzed against water for 72 h by using a dialysis bag (3000 Da) to remove any molecular residual [21] and to obtain the desired PEI1.8K-CD. PEI0.6K-CD and PEI10K-CD were prepared in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversible fluorescence “on-off” process of the PEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A given amount of the aqueous DMSO solution of PEI-CDs was added into a glass tube, which was equipped with two glass duck feets for an inlet and an outlet in a rubber stopper. The glass tube was placed into a water bath, and the temperature of which was controlled at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C. Then CO2 was bubbled through the mixture at about 5–6 bubbles per second under magnetic stirring. At this time, precipitation was observed from the solutions. Bubbling was continued until the PL intensity of PEI-CDs was decreased significantly. Interestingly, CO2 could be removed by heating the turbid solutions of PEI-CDs-CO2 at 65 °C for 30 min with N2 bubbling. After that, the precipitation disappeared and the system became clear again. The trial was considered to be reversible as the fluorescence of the solution could be readily transformed back to its original intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of structure and morphology of the PEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Both the as-prepared CDs and PEI-CDs were characterized by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum, 13C NMR spectrum, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and XPS spectrum. As shown in Fig. 2 a, UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the pristine CDs exhibited two characteristic peaks at 203 nm and 330 nm. The former might be assigned to π-π* transition of the unsaturated conjugated nanocarbon structure, and the latter could be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the CO bond [22,23]. Nevertheless, UV–Vis absorption peaks of the PEI-CDs were all located at 210 nm and 281 nm (Fig. 2a and Fig. S1a). Taking PEI1.8K-CD as an example, the absorption peak of the pristine CDs at 203 nm moved to a higher wavelength of 210 nm in the PEI1.8K-CD due to the surface passivation of the CDs by PEI1.8K, while the peak of the CDs at 330 nm shifted to a lower wavelength of 281 nm owing to the hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl groups of the CDs and secondary amine of the PEI [12], which decreased the energy level of the n-orbital.

As expected, infrared characteristic peaks of the PEIs appeared in the FTIR spectrum of the PEI-CDs (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1b), where the peak at 1643 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of amide CO of the PEI-CDs, and the strong peak at 1563 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibration of NH bonds [14,20], indicating the successful amidation of CDs and PEIs. The PEI-CDs were further confirmed by 13C NMR spectra. The signal at 179.1 ppm was assigned to the stretching vibration of amide CO directly anchored onto the CDs (see Supporting Information Fig. S2) [24]. Moreover, TGA analysis was performed for the CDs and PEI-CDs (see Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information). The weight loss of pristine CDs was about 80 wt% at 500 °C. Since the neat PEI polymer was completely decomposed up to 500 °C, as indicated in Fig. S3 (Supporting Information), mass loss from the PEI-CDs at 500 °C was used to estimate the amount of the polymer that was covalently attached to the surface of CDs. A mass loss of about 14 wt% was observed for the PEI-CDs at 500 °C due to the polymer decomposition [25].

XPS spectrum further confirmed that PEI-CDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements (Fig. 3 a and Fig. S4). The content of each element was given in Table S1 (Supporting Information). It can be seen from Fig. 3b that the fine structure spectrum of C1s exhibited three main peaks at 284.8, 285.7 and 288.2 eV, which was attributed to graphitic (or aliphatic), nitrous, and oxygenated carbon atoms, respectively [15]. The two peaks for N1s (Fig. 3c) were ascribed to two kinds of N atoms in amide group and amine [26]. The spectrum of O1s in the PEI-CDs (Fig. 3d) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.5 (CO) and 532.6 (CO) eV [26]. Therefore, it is appropriate to state that PEIs were chemically bonded on the surface of CDs through amidation reaction.

The morphology and structure of the CDs and PEI-CDs were studied by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). It was found from Fig. 4 a that the CDs were mono-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 3.5 nm, and they had a crystalline structure composed of parallel crystal planes with the lattice spacing of 0.20–0.22 nm (inset of Fig. 4a), which is very close to the (100) diffraction planes of graphite [22]. This result confirms the formation of CDs. On the other hand, the nanoparticles of PEI-CDs exhibited irregular shape and they were a few tens of nanometers in size (Fig. 4b and Fig. S5). Taking PEI1.8K-CD as an example, a lattice structure with about 0.21 nm lattice spacing (Fig. 4b) was observed, which matches well with the (100) diffraction planes of graphite derived from the pristine CDs. This result further indicates that the PEIs were chemically bound on the surface of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CO2 switchable fluorescence of the PEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Solubility of the PEI-CDs was firstly determined in some solvents. It was found that these PEI-CDs were soluble in water, but PEI0.6K-CD and PEI1.8K-CD were very difficult to dissolve in DMSO due to their shorter chain in the ethylenimine unit. Interestingly, all the as-prepared PEI-CDs had good solubility in certain ratios of DMSO-water mixture. Therefore, aqueous DMSO solutions with different amounts of water were chosen as the medium used in this work.

Excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior is a widely observed phenomenon in fluorescent carbon nanomaterials [23]. Indeed, the as-prepared PEI-CDs in this work were found to exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior in aqueous DMSO solutions. As a representative example, the PL spectrum of PEI1.8K-CD (4 mg/ml) in DMSO-water mixture (wt/wt, 1.5/1.0) was recorded and the results were illustrated in Fig. S6. It was shown that within the excitation wavelength from 300 to 520 nm, the corresponding PL peak shifted from 450 to 570 nm for the PEI-CD. All solutions of the PEI-CDs in aqueous DMSO were transparent and showed strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of UV lamp at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 5 ). After bubbling with CO2, the PEI-CDs solutions became turbid (inset of Fig. 5) due to the CO2-induced precipitation of PEI-CDs particles. At this stage, the solutions still exhibited blue luminescence, but the PL intensity was dramatically decreased (Fig. 5). Actually, the emission intensity decreased about 65% after only 20–30s bubbling of CO2. Further bubbling of CO2 did not change the PL intensity of the PEI-CDs solutions any more. The molecular weight of PEIs was found to have an effect on the response time of the excitation and emission intensity for CO2. However, seconds to tens of seconds of CO2 bubbling were generally enough to switch off the fluorescence of the PEI-CDs solutions. In addition, choosing a right ratio of DMSO to water is critical for the successful switching of the fluorescence of different PEI-CDs. We found that 1.2/1.0, 1.5/1.0 and 1.9/1.0 were the best choice for PEI0.6K-CD, PEI1.8K-CD and PEI10K-CD, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for CO2 switchable fluorescence of the PEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a deeper insight into the mechanism for switch on and off of the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs solutions, the precipitation generated by the reaction of PEI1.8K-CD with CO2 in DMSO-water mixture was collected, dried, and then used for 13C NMR measurements in D2O. A new signal at 160.2 ppm was observed and assigned to HCO3 − anion (Fig. 6 a) [27,28], which confirms the formation of polyammonium bicarbonates precipitation. On the other hand, it was found that pH value of the system decreased from 9.3 to 7.5 when the reaction equilibrium was reached after CO2 bubbling. In order to examine the key factor affecting the switch off and on of the system's fluorescence, we investigated the pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CDs solutions. For this purpose, a very small amount of hydrochloric acid was added into aqueous DMSO solution containing PEI1.8K-CD (4 mg/ml) in the absence of CO2 to change solution pH value to 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0 and 9.0 (see Fig. S7), respectively. It was shown that the change in fluorescence intensity of the PEI-CD solution was small with the change of the solution pH value from 7.5 to 9.0, indicating that the change in the system's pH could result in the fluorescence quenching and recovery, but it was not the main driving force for the switch off and on of the system's fluorescence.

Then, we made a contrast test by bubbling CO2, respectively, into aqueous solution of PEI1.8K-CD and aqueous DMSO solution of PEI1.8K-CD at the same PEI-CD content of 4 mg/ml to demonstrate the precipitation effect on the system's fluorescence. It was shown that no precipitation and fluorescence intensity change was observed in aqueous solution of the PEI-CD. However, in aqueous DMSO solution of the PEI-CD, precipitation of polyammonium bicarbonate was formed due to its poor solubility in this mixed solvent, which decreased concentration of the functionalized CD in aqueous DMSO by bubbling of CO2 and led to the switch off of the system's fluorescence. This finding suggests that the precipitation formation of polyammonium bicarbonates in aqueous DMSO solution could play the key role in the switch on and off of the system's fluorescence. Therefore, the fluorescence “on-off” results involve the reversible acid - base reaction of the functionalized CDs with CO2 and the precipitation formation of hydrophilic polyammonium bicarbonates (Fig. 6b). When CO2 was removed by N2 bubbling, the precipitation was returned to the DMSO-water mixture, and thereby the fluorescence was switched on again. Moreover, even without bubbling of CO2, similar changes could be also observed by simply exposing the solution of PEI-CDs to the ambient air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility of the CO2 switchable fluorescence of the PEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The “switch on” process of the fluorescence was confirmed by monitoring 13C NMR spectrum after CO2 was removed by heating the turbid solution of PEI-CD-CO2 at 65 °C for 30 min with N2 bubbling. It was shown that the signal at 160.2 ppm in the 13C NMR spectrum of PEI-CDs disappeared (Fig. 6a). Additionally, no significant changes were observed in both ‘‘on’’ and ‘‘off’’ luminescence intensities within 20 cycles (Fig. 7 ). These results suggest that the reaction between PEI-CDs and CO2 in DMSO-water mixture was reversible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the PEI-CDs for detection of CO2 and SO2
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the above results, we utilized the CO2 responsive PEI-CDs for the detection of CO2 gas. Taking PEI1.8K-CD as an example, the fluorescent emission spectrum of the PEI1.8K-CD in DMSO-water mixture towards different volumes of pure CO2 gas was measured according to the reported procedures [29–32]. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that a marked decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity was observed upon exposure to increasing volumes of CO2 gas at a fixed rate. Then the molar concentrations (mol/L) of H2CO3 (i.e. molar concentration of CO2) in the systems (including the precipitation) were determined from acid-base titration by following the procedures reported by Gennaro et al. [33]. Also, the equivalent concentration of CO2 in ppm was calculated in the systems [34]. It was found that the PL intensity of PEI1.8K-CD solution was linearly decreased with the increase of CO2 concentration, and a good linear correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9951 was obtained (inset of Fig. 8), which indicates that this nanoprobe could be used to monitor the concentration of CO2 quantitatively. The detection limit calculated from the linear equation: LOD = 3σ/k, was 1.89 μM (ca. 45 ppm), where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the slope of the linear equation [31,32]. In addition, according to the approach of Burke et al. [35], the molar concentrations of H2CO3 in aqueous DMSO solution were converted to the corresponding partial pressure of CO2 in the atmosphere by using Henry law: p(CO2) = [H2CO3]/K H, where the concentration of [H2CO3] was expressed in mol/L and K H is Henry coefficient which can be calculated from related phase equilibrium data [36]. In this way, we are able to calculate the content of CO2 in gas mixtures. For example, the content of CO2 in the atmosphere was determined to be 382 ± 3 ppm by the approach developed in this work, which is close to the result (390 ppm) reported in literature [37,38].

SO2 is a significant source of atmospheric pollution [39,40], and it is of critical importance to detect this harmful gas. Since SO2 has stronger acidity than CO2 [41], the PEI-CDs developed here should be able to detect SO2 with higher sensitivity due to the basic components of the nanoprobe. For the detection of trace amounts of SO2 by the strategy described above, we diluted pure SO2 by N2 to obtain different volume fractions of SO2 (0.04%, 0.08%, 0.10%, 0.2%, 0.5%, 0.8% and 1.0%). With PEI1.8K-CD as an example again, the PL intensity of PEI1.8K-CD solution decreased with the increase of molar concentrations of SO2, and a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9968) was found (see Fig. S8). The detection limit of this system was calculated to be 0.19 μM (ca. 4.0 ppm), which was about 10 times lower than that for CO2 detection. Thus, the PEI-CDs could also be used as fluorescent probes to detect SO2 in the atmosphere. In addition, considering the acid-base characteristics for the detection of CO2 and SO2 by the PEI-CDs, the strategy described above must be able to detect other more acidic gases.

In recent years, the detection of CO2 or SO2 has also been reported based on CDs and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). For example, Sun et al. [10] developed SO2 responsive cyanine dye functionalized CDs and then used them for selective and quantitative determination of SO2, bisulfite (HSO3 −) and sulfite (SO3 2−). Although these probes exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity for gaseous SO2, their bonding with SO2 is too strong to be reversible. Jimenez-Cadena et al. [42] reported that CNTs functionalized with organic polymers by non-covalent interactions showed high selectivity to CO2 or SO2. The non-covalent functionalization did not usually require expensive materials and fabrication technique, while their stability is still a problem. Star et al. [43] and Cid et al. [44] found that chemically functionalized field effect transistors by a specific receptor based on single-walled carbon nanotubes could be used for the selective detection of CO2 and SO2, respectively. Indeed, these probes show high selectivity, but the functionalization process was tedious. Compared with the above investigations, the PEI-CDs nanoprobes developed in this work exhibited advantages of cheap and easy preparation, stability, and reversibility.

In summary, we successfully prepared CO2 responsive PEI-CDs via chemically bonding of branched PEIs to the surface of CDs. All the obtained PEI-CDs were photoluminescent, and their fluorescence was switchable in DMSO-water mixture by alternatively bubbling and removal of CO2. The CO2 switchable mechanism may be understood from the fact that the PEI-CDs could react with CO2 to form polyammonium bicarbonates, which were precipitated to decrease the concentration of PEI-CDs in DMSO-water mixture. When CO2 was removed, the precipitation was returned to the mixture again, and the fluorescence was restored. Importantly, the PEI-CDs were demonstrated to be excellent fluorescent probes for the quantitative detection of CO2 and SO2 in aqueous solution and in the air. We believe that the strategy developed in this work may provide a new path for preparing “smart” materials used in the field of sensing and photocatalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21673068 and 21603063) and the key Scientific Research Project Fund of Education Department of Henan Province (No. 16A150014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

Providing UV–Vis absorption spectra, FTIR spectra, 13C NMR spectra, thermogravimetric analysis, XPS spectra, HRTEM images, photoluminescent spectra, the pH effect on the fluorescence intensity, and SO2 gas detection.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.02.090.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective detection of phosphate in very complicated matrixes with an off-on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted carbon dots
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots decorated on graphene: a novel all-carbon hybrid electrocatalyst for enhanced oxygen reduction reaction
Sulfated carbon quantum dots as efficient visible-light switchable acid catalysts for room-temperature ring-opening reactions
Super-resolution optical fluctuation bio-imaging with dual-color carbon nanodots
Bright-yellow-emissive N-doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
Determination of gaseous sulfur dioxide and its derivatives via fluorescence enhancement based on cyanine dye functionalized carbon nanodots
Organic amine-grafted carbon quantum dots with tailored surface and enhanced photoluminescence properties
pH-switched luminescence and sensing properties of a carbon dot-polyaniline composite
Photoluminescent organosilane-functionalized carbon dots as temperature probes
Hyperbranched polymer functionalized carbon dots with multistimuli-responsive property
Tunable amphiphilicity and multifunctional applications of ionic-liquid-modified carbon quantum dots
CO2-triggered switchable solvents, surfactants, and other materials
Selective separation of aliphatic and aromatic amines with CO2 switchable ionic liquids aqueous two-phase systems
Polyethylenimine-assisted extraction of α-tocopherol from tocopherol homologues and CO2-triggered fast recovery of the extractant
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper Ions
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Preparation and properties of EDC/NHS mediated crosslinking poly (gamma-glutamic acid)/epsilon-polylysine hydrogels
Observation of lasing emission from carbon nanodots in organic solvents
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
Synthesis of amide-functionalized cellulose esters by olefin cross-metathesis
Covalent functionalization of single-walled carbon nanotubes with thermoresponsive core cross-linked polymeric micelles
Carbon dots as fluorescent sensor for detection of explosive nitrocompounds
